title: Ruthea gummifera
taxonomy: Ruthea gummifera is a species within the family Rutaceae. This family is commonly known as the citrus or rue family, which comprises a diverse group of flowering plants. Rutaceae is further classified under the order Sapindales, which includes other families like Sapindaceae and Anacardiaceae. The order Sapindales is part of the clade Rosids, a large group of flowering plants. The Rosids clade is a subgroup of the larger clade of flowering plants called the Angiosperms.
uses: Ruthea gummifera, also known as the Gum Bush, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's gum, which is obtained from its stem, is believed to have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties, making it useful for wound healing and treating skin conditions. In some cultures, the Gum Bush is also used to treat respiratory issues, such as coughs and colds, due to its expectorant properties. Additionally, the plant's gum has been traditionally used as a treatment for sore throats and mouth ulcers. However, more research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of using Ruthea gummifera for medicinal purposes.

parts: Ruthea gummifera is a plant native to Australia, and its medicinal properties are primarily derived from the gum resin it secretes. The plant's gum resin is rich in polyphenols and other bioactive compounds, which have been found to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Ruthea gummifera's medicinal parts include the gum resin, which has been traditionally used by indigenous Australians to treat various ailments. This tea is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties and is used to treat various ailments, including fever and rheumatism. intro: Ruthea gummifera, also known as the gum resin tree or gum-resin tree, is a plant species native to the western Himalayas and the Indian subcontinent. The health benefits of Ruthea gummifera include its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which are thought to aid in wound healing and reduce symptoms of respiratory and gastrointestinal issues. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, terpenoids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditionally, Ruthea gummifera is used to make herbal preparations such as decoctions, infusions, and ointments, which are used to treat a range of health issues, including coughs, colds, and skin conditions.
